metric_value: Accountants, architects, doctors, insurance brokers, lawyers, plumbers, undertakers, and all Aberdeen employees are in the Professional Services Business
notes: Everyone at Aberdeen is always selling Aberdeen services at all times

metric_value: Clients hire on trust; they never get to see results until after a project is complete
notes: Being an Aberdeen member starts the trust-building process; publications create tangible evidence

metric_value: Trust means always being honest and objective; telling client 'You are the best' is easy but if market share declines, trust is broken
notes: Trust requires listening and asking questions, not jumping to conclusions and dictating recommendations

metric_value: Trust means respecting client confidence; clients will not trust you in future if you break another firm's NDA, even in your enthusiasm
notes: Exact language: 'even in your enthusiasm to help your client'

metric_value: Trust means doing what you say you will do and quickly disclosing what Aberdeen cannot do
notes: Explicit: promptly disclose Aberdeen limitations rather than overpromising

metric_value: A successful 'first date' project — such as preparing a Profile or giving a speech — can build the trust necessary to move to the next level
notes: Explicitly called a 'first date' project; low-risk entry point for new client relationships

metric_name: Product Life Cycle: Research stage key question
metric_value: What functionality, distribution channels, and other attributes (pricing) must our product have to be successful? What market segments should we target?
notes: Stage 1 of the 6-stage Aberdeen PLC framework

metric_name: Product Life Cycle: Product Intro stage key question
metric_value: How can Aberdeen better educate the market about the value and advantages our products and services provide users? With whom should we partner?
source_page: Key Client Questions: Product Intro stage
notes: Stage 2 — education and partnering questions

metric_name: Product Life Cycle: Early market response stage key question
metric_value: We have heard the initial market response to our value proposition. Could Aberdeen tell us what it really means and where we should invest our resources for success?
source_page: Key Client Questions: Early response stage
notes: Stage 3 — interpreting initial market response

metric_name: Product Life Cycle: Crossing the Chasm stage key question
metric_value: What factors are stopping prospective buyers from acquiring products from us? How can we cross the chasm?
source_page: Key Client Questions: Crossing the Chasm slide
notes: References Geoffrey Moore's Crossing the Chasm concept explicitly; Stage 4

metric_name: Product Life Cycle: Rapid Market Growth stage key question
metric_value: How can we differentiate our products and services so that the majority of IS decision makers will see us as the safe choice for the future?
source_page: Key Client Questions: Rapid Market Growth stage
notes: Stage 5 — becoming the safe/default choice

metric_name: Product Life Cycle: Maturity stage key question (1)
metric_value: How does our firm become or remain #1 in our marketplace?
notes: Stage 6a — market leadership question

metric_name: Product Life Cycle: Maturity stage key question (2)
metric_value: What additional products, services, distribution channels, target markets should we add to further fuel growth rates and outflank competition? Who should we consider acquiring?
source_page: Key Client Questions: Maturity growth stage
notes: Stage 6b — expansion and acquisition question

metric_name: Product Life Cycle: Decline stage key question (1)
metric_value: How do we maintain the loyalty of our installed base and hold off competition from alternative technologies?
notes: Stage 7a — installed base retention question

metric_name: Product Life Cycle: Decline stage key question (2)
metric_value: How do we motivate our installed base to continue to invest in our products to maximize the return on their past investments?
notes: Stage 7b — installed-base monetization; matches what the text calls 'Rebuild Momentum' stage

metric_name: Product Life Cycle: Rebuild momentum stage key question
metric_value: What should we do to rebuild momentum? And if we cannot, how can we best extend product's life while using key assets to enter new, high growth markets?
source_page: Key Client Questions: Rebuild Momentum stage
notes: Stage 8 — graceful decline and pivot question

metric_name: Critical complaint about Aberdeen from suppliers
metric_value: Suppliers find it difficult to know how to do business with Aberdeen
source_page: Meeting and Closing Business For The First Time slide
notes: Explicit acknowledgment of the main barrier to client acquisition

metric_name: The power of optimism in closing
metric_value: If not optimistic, odds are very slim that the firm will engage Aberdeen at end of this meeting; do not write a Profile on a product that is inappropriate for the market
notes: Analysts should not manufacture enthusiasm to gain short-term revenues

metric_name: Profile close script at supplier-initiated meetings
metric_value: Aberdeen can help leverage your marketing messages through an independent perspective in the form of a Profile; fee approximately $15,000; if findings contrary to expectations, no requirement to publish
source_page: How Successful Analysts Close: Profile slide
notes: Key pitch elements: independent perspective, $15,000 fee, no-publish option if findings negative

metric_value: Aberdeen does not churn out paper; $15,000/year: 2 consulting days on-site, quarterly telephone updates, telephone inquiries, all practice area publications
source_page: How Successful Analysts Close: Personal Retainer slide
notes: Explicit $15,000/year price point with specific deliverables

metric_value: I honestly believe you are misunderstanding how real IS decision makers view this issue; RAMP: $60,000-$100,000; initial findings 30 days; complete 60 days
source_page: How Successful Analysts Close: RAMP slide
notes: RAMP is for when the supplier does not know what it does not know; 1.5-hour site interviews with highest-potential prospects

metric_value: Aberdeen will not work on projects where there is a potential conflict of interest between two different clients
notes: Explicitly stated in proposal general provisions as a contractual clause

metric_value: The more suppliers you meet with, the more projects you will obtain; the more Aberdeen publications you author, the more projects you will obtain
notes: Effect is cumulative; start early and often; also includes media quotes, speeches, and trade articles

metric_value: Year 1: Profile with web rights $25K; Year 2: Analysts Plus Service + Profile + Professional services $65K; Year 3: Personal Retainer + two Profiles + RAMP $150K+
notes: Three-year revenue progression model for a single supplier relationship

metric_value: Return press calls Wednesday afternoon and Thursday mornings; prepare three Viewpoints on distributed computing; mail to 650 most influential executives on six-week cycle
notes: Answer prospect calls generated by mailing 3PM-6PM on Friday; commit to fixed prices on phone

metric_value: Cover letter + Intro + Background + Scope of Work + Work Tasks + Cost and Duration + Expenses + General Provisions + Staff Responsibility + Acceptance + Appendix A (Aberdeen blurb) + Appendix B (Resumes)
source_page: Anatomy of An Aberdeen Written Proposal slide
notes: Even though most projects are proposed and closed verbally; written proposal for administrative sign-off

metric_value: Expenses are invoiced separately; Aberdeen takes no markup on expenses
source_page: Written Proposal: Cost and Duration slide
notes: Professional services fees, reprint prices, and web license fees listed separately from expenses

metric_value: Aberdeen will begin work based on verbal approval and will patiently wait for formal Purchase Order
notes: States expiration date; suggests start date to move from planning to action and revenue generation

metric_name: Aberdeen server file paths for proposals
metric_value: Aberdeen blurb: \\Aberdeen1\Marketing\Blurbs\; Resumes: \\Aberdeen1\Marketing\Bios\
source_page: Written Proposal: Appendix A and Appendix B slides
notes: Infrastructure detail: file server \\Aberdeen1\ is the internal marketing assets server; blurb often needs reformatting for printer

metric_value: Ask for business — potential clients are expecting you to; it is the only way a business transaction can be started
notes: Framed as conclusion: everybody's approach will be slightly different, but you cannot sit in the office and hope business will develop

metric_value: The best prospect is a satisfied client; follow your satisfied clients from job to job
notes: Better first project results = more projects without proposal requests

metric_name: Individual business planning: four revenue pipeline categories
metric_value: Proposals/Prospects 90+ days; Work in Progress 45-90 days; Invoices Out 0-45 days; $ Received: Current
notes: Ratio for $200K quarterly target: Proposals $55K-75K; WIP $35K; Invoices $35K; $ Received $0 at quarter start

metric_value: Ratio for $200K quarterly target: Proposals/Prospects $55K-75K; Work in Progress $35K; Invoices $35K
notes: The $ Received fills in as quarter progresses; target is $200K per analyst per quarter

metric_name: Architecture suitability for use case principle
metric_value: Based on company's Product Life Cycle stage, current industry dynamics, and strengths/weaknesses of supplier's offering, ask questions rather than give instant analysis
notes: Asking questions shows maturity and continues trust building; gives opportunity to more carefully evaluate supplier strategy

metric_value: Show Time — Please present to the class; analysts describe a supplier, their messages, analyst analysis, and verbally propose an Aberdeen service to the group
source_page: Starting the Business Development Process / Verbal Proposal Time slides
notes: Interactive pedagogical technique; the Session 4 deck has two role-play exercises: practice introduction and verbal proposal

metric_name: Publication as trust-building and revenue driver
metric_value: If not working on a project, you should be writing; best writing is communicating a vision of market dynamics or writing a Profile or Impact on spec
notes: Spec publications (written without client) build future revenue; send to press, suppliers, and users

metric_name: The thin line between education and free consulting
metric_value: Nothing wrong with saying: You have been seeking my advice for 6 months; we need to formalize this in a Retainer service or another Aberdeen product
source_page: Thin Line In The Sand slide
notes: Implicit cross-reference to Session 2 (same principle stated in both training sessions)
